item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first images is public domain.

The second image is from a series of videos on the fabled Apollo 20 landing, hoax videos posted on Youtube in 2007. As there was never any indication that these images were released under CC (which was not helped by how Rutledge refused to admit that the videos were a hoax), the images are believed to still belong to their copyright owners and as such have since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of those moments you wish you could upvote comments…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is genuine brick-shitting fuel, another one of my upvotes has found a new location. I am a sucker for space stuff, have it, and a sticker of a hamburger.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have more than earned my upvote. When I reaized what was going on, I do believe that bricks were shat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had my +1 at "[DATA EXPUNGED][DATA EXPUNGED][DATA EXPUNGED]"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, this article wasn't really doing much for me. I really dug the "ongoing containment breach" angle, and it's nowhere near what I'd call a bad article, so I wasn't going to downvote it but, since it didn't really manage to pull me in all that much, I wasn't going to upvote it either. But, see, that last thing you put behind the spoiler there packs a MUCH bigger wallop than most of what I can see in the article itself, and I feel like it doesn't come through at all. Maybe I could have figured it out after a while but, as it is, I wouldn't have bothered to give it any more attention than the second read I just gave it and my going through the comments.

So, I don't know if you're planning on making any more changes to it but, I would very much suggest that you hint at that particular bit a whole lot more, since it instantly makes the skip much more intriguing.

Again, maybe you have no intentions of changing it any further, but letting that angle show itself just a tad bit more would certainly make it work a lot, lot better for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't the article be tagged with "meta", then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And if this incredibly powerful alien species hates the Foundation, then why hasn't it just flown over and blown it up?

Perhaps they did.
Perhaps they "did."
Perhaps they knew the Foundation had this.
Perhaps the Foundation made them jump the gun.
Perhaps they noted someone else was about to do it and lost interest.
Or perhaps they will simply drop SCP-████ and wait for it to do the job. It's not like 2777 can't, but probably has bigger fishes to fry. In any case, it's nice (?) to know that you have a hypertechnological species of God-Ships waiting to imagine all sorts of niceties for us to write about them.

By the way, I love this SCP, but when I was reading it for the first time, I could only think of Rendezvous with Rama.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. Yup.

2.

+ take a peek into chubert's shitty headcanon here
- k thanks bye

Actually, that's not meant to imply that each world has its own Foundation. Its meant to imply that the extraterrestrial race already knew about the Foundation, and already really didn't like it. The reason for this being—whenever the Foundation encounters any form of extraterrestrial life, what does it do? Lock it up, test it for science. But then, there are more questions. Isn't this an extraordinarily ancient race? How does a ship that's been crashed on earth for tens of thousands of years know that everybody hates the Foundation? And if this incredibly powerful alien species hates the Foundation, then why hasn't it just flown over and blown it up?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A battle is coming. There are still secrets here: that which a council of the unholy figure would seek to conceal.

Is that a meta effect?

He takes upon himself the sign of evil that is hated and despised throughout all the worlds.

The Foundation symbol? It'd be interesting if every world with a civilization had its own Foundation, and they all used the same symbol.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Word choice is just that, a choice. Subject to all the usual personal preferences.

The fundamental question is which is more important, accuracy or readability? I heavily favor the second, because at the end of the day we aren't really writing technical papers. Feel free to disagree, I won't take it personally.

For context, I am also opposed to grammar for the sake of grammar and (error in the opposite direction) exhaustive phonetic transcripts of an accent. Ideally, fiction should be written as close as possible to the way the target audience speaks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't understand why you don't like the use of "alfa" and "audial," which are both the right words used correctly. Yes, "alfa" will read like a typo if you don't know how the NATO phonetic alphabet works, but that doesn't mean that it's not the right word. Am I supposed to use the wrong word, then? If correct usage detracts from a reader's enjoyment, then I'm going with correct usage, especially in an article. This sounds like complaining about the sentence, "It is I." Yes, it sounds wrong, but it's correct, and it's not the writer's problem if the sentence is framed that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like the alfa either. (all arguments have been listed)

I did not realy like the Foramt screw in the end. I can see the intention (and I don't think that second footnote is bad), but It kinda ran away. seems to similar to clefs 001 (and the project:thaumiel about it)

I would have liked it more if you just left it hanging.
the event goes on, but if there is no one left whe cares to write about it…

would have been an upvote if not for that (well when the contest is over I will upvote. on the contest I'm harsher.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Empty vacuum" is redundant, a vacuum is empty by definition.

While "Alfa" is technically correct, it reads as a typo. I am all for accuracy and verisimilitude in most cases, but in this instance the realism distracts from the reader's enjoyment. At least it did for this reader.

Same for "audial". It's technically correct, but looks like a typo and disrupts the flow of reading like a typo.

The radio chatter is cliché and instead of personalizing the team made them into caricatures I couldn't care about.

I love the breach-in-progress concept; but this doesn't do anything interesting with the angle. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty dang epic

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the article, but stand by my earlier assertion in chat that the pic looks like a flattened Barn Owl.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is the best spacecraft entry. Also, this is the first time I have ever upvoted an article before reaching the Item Class. The idea of something currently breaching containment is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's well written, and though the idea is somewhat generic the article as a whole is unique enough to me to deserve an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well we did....But that's hardly relevant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno. It kinda managed to intrigue me, exploration log and all.
Of course, it more or less follows the rails of every single "downed creepy UFO thingie" (Alien franchise included), but still, got me wondering about its origins, in a good way :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to upvote just because I like seeing what a breach in-progress.

It could be shorter, but I upvoted when I saw the header and the rest was good enough that I didn't undo it.

Edit: It wasn't really bad at all, I mean what issues I had weren't severe enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the ending, and the SCP talking to the reader.

(Also, that shouldn't be under a collapsible.)

The rest I wanted to downvote for, because it was that generic. Especially this whole paragraph:

SCP-2000-1 is suspended at the “center” (as denoted by the structure of SCP-2000) of the central chamber. SCP-2000-1 is a humanoid entity that bears visual similarity to a male human child, although x-ray CT and PET scans have revealed several internal anatomical anomalies (see Document-2000-A3 for further details). Telepathic communications, presumed to be from SCP-2000-1, can be heard inside the central chamber. SCP-2000-1's breathing, heartbeat and metabolic rates are extremely slow. Furthermore, SCP-2000-1 has not been recorded to move, suggesting that, while SCP-2000-1 is conscious, its body is in a state of stasis.

Yeah. Whatever. Faminepulse's aborted reality bender was much cooler.

I also find the reality bending events themselves underwhelming — although the picture is very cool.

The exploration logs could be cut entirely — I got nothing out of them whatsoever. They just unfavorably compare to the many awesome logs written elsewhere on the site.

Balances out to no vote. To me, this is half of a good SCP with decently written generic placeholder material filling up the other half. Would be happy to upvote a version where both halves match in quality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: and i am not taking a stab at your comment :p. maybe we can put away the imaginary knives now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm only distantly familiar with X-com. I just like unconventional aliens! Also, I'm not taking a stab at the article or anything…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: well, unlike x-com, which i am admittedly not very familiar with, the alien is really not the point in this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
its one of those words.
amnestic
amnesiac
tic
iac
no big deal for me, i dont read words in depth. I read both words as this:
Its memory go-away and we have alot of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the people always seem to ignore this:

choose one: [A] [B] [C]

options seem to be a thing industries dont want to give.
for example simcity "needs" online, my dishwasher had hidden buttons, spore has only easy and boring and not the fun we were promised (still fun though)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, according to available dev diaries/commentary, they ran into problems during playtesting, because some of the "evil geometry" creatures had no intuitive cues as to their "vision cones", hearing ability, etc., and players could not tell the "shooty side of alien" from the "side you're supposed to sneak up on".

Which says sad things about modern gamers :-).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, going from malevolent blocks to humanoids… Why not just give up if your aliens aren't going to be interesting?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ever saw the "old-old" XCOM trailers ? I mean the ones with the old "blocky metal aliens pretending to be humans" design which they, sadly, dropped in favor of more banal one.

There's a lot of fun (and creep) to be had with non-humanoid and pseudo-humanoid creatures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That looks pretty good! Really good idea, lovin' it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're technically right, but I use amnesiac anyway, just because it seems pretty ingrained in site culture anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would be Keter if they had a new documentation file, but they don't, because it broke containment literally a few hours ago, whereas all of Eskobar's revision-thingies are all written some time after the fact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't really want to clutter up the note with times, but I did add a few words on how long they lasted, so if you subtract, you'll get the time they were sent it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Correct me if I'm wrong, but aren't the things you give to people to make them lose their memory called amnestics?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A day in the life of the Foundation…

Why isn't the document updated to show its Keter status like Eskobar's submission?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: /me shrug. Human-looking aliens. I felt that it'd be just a bit too cheesy if I made it a really obvious tentacled alien, or something. Maybe they can shapeshift and it chose this form to elicit more sympathy?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this one in the chat, and I like it here. I dig the "real time" feel of the document that achieves what the narrative is going for without wandering too far into esoteric weird shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …huh. It may just be me, but I saw the oblong-shape jutting from the ground to be the 'Sword in the Stone' as it were, and the black-box names are all long enough to fit the words British, Arthur and Mordred.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. No vote yet because I'm not voting until editing closes, but probably upvote coming. The sense of dread builds nicely throughout, though I'm a little iffy on 2000-1. Why does it look like a human child?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First part makes sense. I googled it and looked around, but I could find any examples. Glad I didn't just go and edit it.

I didn't finish reading it, so I didn't know it was being hacked. Also makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another great entry! One thing thought…

At 0723 hours, all stealth drones were destroyed.

This is good and all, but you don't mention when the drones were sent in. Why state a time when they were destroyed if you're not going to also say when they went in?
Aside from that, love it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty dang good, I must say. I'm impressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, because in the NATO phonetic alphabet, it's spelled Alfa, because for non French or English speaker, you wouldn't be able to tell that it's pronounced as an "f" sound with the "ph." Also no, the multiple [DATA EXPUNGED]s are intentional. The document is getting hijacked; it's not supposed to make sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alfa—> Alpha, and "[DATA EXPUNGED][DATA EXPUNGED][DATA EXPUNGED]. What more are they hiding from you?" should only have one '[DATA EXPUNGED],' since they're consecutive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All fixed; thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dig this. I thought it was going to be a 2001 star child and I don't think it is. It seems like the kid is very downplayed and not actually that important, like hes maybe just a mouthpiece? I did, however, notice this line, "Reality warping events are typically restructuring, and replace the inside of a chamber with a newly fabricated setting." This seemed a little clumsy to me, it might be better with no comma after "restructuring" and replacement of "replace" with "replacement of", no syntax pun intended. Also, ultrasonography wouldn't work in a vacuum, no air, no medium, no sound. Maybe a PET scan? Finally, its "metabolic rate" not "metabolism rate", and saying that it, and breathing and hearth rate, were "retarded" implies that they slowed from a higher point, not that it is slow compared to a regular humans. Those would be my suggestions, but this totally tastes like Vonnegut, which I like. Also makes me thing of a relation to Clefs 001 proposal.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: sorry? where'd you get that impression?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, we fucked up Camelot real good, didn't we?
